TL; DR: Beginner friendly (at least for 70% content), best if you have a bit of programming knowledge (C/C++, Java). Might need some time for the library.

The new course opened in 2025 Fall to replace 1021 turtles.
My background is quite a lot: ICT C/C++, Python, Java, so you could imagine a lot of concepts are actually duplicated.
Shout-out for my fellow ASSO mates.

Python is one of the important programming languages in multiple fields, even helping you in basic calculus (trust me), I would say it is worth the content and worth the time.  

### Overall topics:
Basic Syntax
Operation
Branching: if-statements (and its extend like if-else, elif etc), switch statement
Looping: For-loop, while / do-while
List
Tuple
Dictionary
Function
Recursion
OOP

Numpy
Pandas
MatPlotlib

### Lab: must attend for attendance, it's not a 3 hr long speech but <1hr so it's fine. The rest of the time is for QnA.
### PAs: I recommend just do not start right away once it is out but wait 1-2 days for it to settle, a bit more convenient than going back-and-forth with the skeleton (drafted) codes.

comment_teaching:
I’d say he’s top 10 in all professors I’ve met, but there are things that can be done better.

Good: makes sure you understand, reminds you of details you may not find elsewhere. I do not want to keep saying the good things because you can see it in almost all other reviews, I am here to talk about my honest feelings as a total newbie to programming.

Bad: pretends to focus a lot on exam (more on grading), but not about the content, just about some boring syntax you can totally avoid, forcing us to listen to something that is not useful anywhere outside of this course, just for him to make crazy papers to trick us. (For example when you really write code there are many ways to do something and you can just pick one way that you like, but he forces you to remember the syntax to all the writing methods and may ask you about how to rewrite a program using other syntax doing the same thing, which I think is totally useless)

I think for a new programmer, the most important thing is to hone a programming mindset and problem-solving mindset using codes. It is very different whether you can write codes or you can read and understand those codes. I’d say the lecturer spams a lot of codes without giving us sufficient motivation. He basically goes like “Show time” and then show us what happens. He has good explanation only on understand understanding the basic concepts but not on honing problem solving techniques. What I mean is that he always walks through why the program works, but never talks about why the program is designed that way and how to come up with that.

Of course I understand that he gives us a lot of self practice, and we only learn by doing, but it will also be better if experts like him can share more with us about how he approaches questions. (I’m sure he can do it well because he is a good story teller, nothing can be easier than just walking through what he has in mind with us) When the focus is about explaining why the program works, it is not really worth it to take lessons because I can just read the notes and run through it line by line (the notes are made by him too, and are good enough to a level that can replace him…), I think insights and thinking are much more precious and should be emphasised a lot more.

I believe this is important, especially for an introductory course (for courses like COMP 2711H, professor never talks about problem-solving and only discusses the beauty of the theorem and the intuition, that is totally fine because it is not an introductory course and we can learn about doing on our own, I am just claiming that it is not appropriate for the very first course in anything like programming)
comment_workload:
2 PA
5 lab exercises
Lab tutorials require attendance (non-sense)
Sometimes non-sense,, too much involvement in resolving very stupid problems rather than actually learning.
Also, much time is spent on dealing with exams with scenarios that never happen in the real world. That kills a lot of time and interest as some exam questions are pure non sense, have some memorise a lot of extra things just to tackle this exam and those have nothing to do with my programming proficiency.

For a total python newbie like me, each lab took me about 2-3 hours to complete.

PA is the ultimate devil.
PA 1 is supposed to be easy from the content covered, but turned out to be extremely tedious.
It took me 4 hours to write all the codes, and 6 hours for me to finish debugging…
First of all, as a person being “introduced” to python, it is really insane to deal with such a high intensity exercise.
However, most of the time it’s not about you not knowing some concepts, but it is about you’re not knowing something they never teach about how python works.
Second, you actually do not know what you’re doing. You are only writing some functions for them to do the work and you never have the big picture of what you are actually trying to do. They will throw a lot of arbitrary names and variables in the code but never explains what they mean. I don’t even know the type of some particular variables and have to test them out using the output (and of course when you talk about reading codes, it’s another level of trouble).
Also, throughout the process you hardly can learn about anything, and you cannot use AI because they have a very strong plagiarism platform that can check.
Usually after spending hours into doing something and finally making it, one feels rewarding as I feel like they have challenged their limits successfully and should see some evidence of improvement. However, that can never be the case here. The only thing I feel is I can finally get rid of this terrible stuff. I cannot appreciate my hard work because it is only to get through all those clumsy details and is not worth my time and effort at all except the 25% course grade.

Luckily, my major is not CS or DSCT, so this will be my only programming course at HKUST and I will never do it anymore.
comment_grading:
Mid term:
The most terrible mid term exam I’ve ever had, even crazier than real analysis, HHM’s multivariable calculus or anything. First, if you need to think, then you won’t be able to finish the paper. Secondly, if you need time to understand their instructions, you won’t be able to finish the paper. Thirdly, if you will be careless under time constraints, I can guarantee you that the number of mistakes you will make in this paper will be much greater than what you do for other paper.
Basically, what is tested has absolutely nothing to do with what is taught. The whole paper is an IQ test about programming, I can guarantee you that it has very little to do with how hard-working you are, as the sole purpose of the paper is just to give you a hard time.
For the T/F questions, some questions are out of syllabus and the concepts were never mentioned in any occasions, nor are they important, whether you know them or not has nothing to do with whether you are a good programmer.
And then the next question asks us to implement them line by line, excuse me, why don’t you just ask us the final output? What’s the point?
And the long questions were basically disaster, they forced you to use particular methods by limiting your number of lines to the answers, which is so stupid because a key part of programming is about algorithmic creativity, his practice of limiting our creativity is extremely discouraging and inappropriate for any purpose as an introductory programming course. Also, the number of questions and reading we need to do exceeded a normal time limit outrageously, come on it is not 2012H, it’s so pointless that the mean last year is 55 and they probably think it’s not low enough and decided to add two more questions to our paper, and of course our paper was much harder than last semester as well, which makes it extremely devastating to even take the exam. It is definitely the worst midterm I have ever taken.
Note: the reason why I say he pretends to focus on exam is that he always say “in the exam I can ask you …”, but he actually never does. All he asks are the Demon questions that anre extremely hard to come up with as an intro, and he has never offered any clues to help us with the exam. All he says is the basics of the basics and won’t help you get more than 30 marks in the exam.
To be honest I understand why he does this because so many people are already experienced and he does not want to make the paper too easy so those people get full marks easily, but he fails to accommodate major students’ needs and tricking us and showing off and telling us how weak we are become his whole purpose given the ratio of demon questions and terrible time constraint. This is pure nonsense

comment_grading:
Laboratory exercises 10%
Programming assignment 1 12%
Programming assignment 2 13%
Midterm examination 25%
Final examination 40%

To get points for the lab, you are requied to attend the lab and finish the requirement / program, and submit it to the ZINC (automatic grading system) on or before the deadline as stated on the lab page. There are 10 lab exercises, only 5 of them will be graded. Each graded lab is worth 10 points. Only the best 4 out of the 5 graded labs will count towards final grade. No late lab assignment will be accepted.


Each lab attendance will be awarded 1 point. Since there are a total of 10 lab meetings, the maximum attendance points you can earn are 10.


To earn attendance points for each lab, you need to answer questions from the teaching assistants during your lab time. To make sure you stay for the first 60 minutes, please use the iPRS to answer all the questions. You won't be penalized for wrong answers, so we encourage you to try answering everything. At the end of the lab, your teaching assistant will randomly select half of the questions to check, and you'll earn 1 attendance point if you answered all of those. If you run into any technical issues that prevent you from submitting your answers using iPRS, please inform the teaching assistants before the lab ends. They won't be able to assist you if you wait until after the lab, and you might lose your lab attendance score as a result.

comment_content:
The title of this course is kind of a scam. This really isn't an "introduction" to python programming. If this is your first time programming, I highly suggest to drop this course and take COMP 1021 + 1028 instead to fulfill the requirements.

The content covers your standard topics of python and programming such as control flow, conditional logic, loops, data types etc. It then covers some data analysis libraries i.e. numpy, pandas and matplotlib which are useful if you are going to take AI courses in the future.

However, personally, I think some of the content chosen is of a very poor standard for an introduction course. I feel like recursion as well as the data analysis libraries aren't really very beginner level concepts. In fact, I felt some useful content such as exception handling, string manipulation, file handling etc. were left out which is much more essential than something like matplotlib for e.g.

Also pandas and matplotlib might as well have not been in the syllabus because barely anything was covered for them. It was basically a "just read the documentation bro" chapter and that's how the exam questions were set from it as well.
comment_teaching:
I did not attend a single lecture because they were boring as fuck if you already knew python well. Also I just find it much faster to read the notes and practice yourself than attend lectures but that's just my preference.

However, I did attend the labs (cuz mandatory attendance) and I'll say that the teaching was fucking dogshit, not cuz the TAs couldn't teach, but cuz of what they actually taught. As beginners, I think it is much more important to focus on how to use programming concepts to solve problems rather than focusing on how they work in depth. Yet all the lab quizzes tested dumbass knowledge like what the output of a confusing bundle of if-statements should be etc. This was useful for me, as a programmer with a solid python background, as it solidified my understanding of python as a programming language, but if you never coded before, this is just useless knowledge.

The self tests suffered from the same issues, they just tested in depth internal working of python code, not how to write python code to solve specific problems. At least the uploaded some practice problems, but that was like only 1 week before the final exam. They should have uploaded those problems way before; I think that would have helped the true beginners a lot more.
comment_workload:
Ok my grade for workload is for people for whom this course is actually an "introduction" to python. If it is, you have to dedicate hours to solving the lab exercises and programming assignments. To prepare for exams, I would suggest solving some easy leetcode problems using python as that would reenforce your python problem solving abilities.

If this course isn't an introduction to python for you, the workload will be much less, probably an A- grade, but the exams will be challenging nonetheless so still prepare seriously, especially if you are not that good at problem solving using code.

comment_content: Others have already covered the syllabus, so I'll just talk about my thoughts on it. Having already known Python before this course, I was surprised at how in-depth they went with every piece of syntax. There are situations you don't normally see in real-life programming covered just so that you know exactly what every keyword does, and what happens when you put them all together. This is up to personal preference; some people may like this, others may not. But you can't argue that this approach does train more competent programmers, even if it is tedious and difficult to master.
comment_teaching:
Desmond's teaching style is...entertaining, and also easy to understand. He draws diagrams showing how the variables store data so that you won't mess up the concepts. I really like that he uploads Zoom videos, which have much better audio quality than the typical RVC videos, and tbh I don't really go to class - I just watch the videos sped up. But the freedom of being able to pause anytime to take notes and think through the content is very helpful.

I personally haven't approached him to ask any questions, but judging by how I often see him talking to students, he's very willing to help if you run into any problems. The anonymous Piazza forum on Canvas also allows you to ask questions if you don't like talking to people face-to-face.
comment_workload:
It's no secret that this course's content is quite challenging, especially to people who have never touched programming before. Even though I've learned Python before, I felt that this course hit my sweet spot of just enough challenge that I really have to think to solve the problems. Plus there are weekly Labs (shorter exercises, still challenging) and Programming Assignments (much longer exercises, several times longer than labs, difficulty is a bit harder than labs). So quite a bit of difficult work to tackle.

It might be a good idea to get yourself familiar with some programming fundamentals first before taking this course. But as someone who took it my very first semester in uni, I think anyone can do so too; it's not the type of course you need to wait further into your academic career before taking.
comment_grading: The grading is fair. You make a mistake in your work, you get marks deducted for it. You need to put quite a lot of effort and study all the details of the syntax meticulously if you want a high score, like >90%. But if you are just aiming for around 80% or higher, it should be pretty doable as long as you have had enough practice and are familiar with the language.

For students who never programmed before, you may feel hard to finish all assignments. But trust me, you will feel really good if you spend 6 hours and finally finish your codes and make the program runnable. You will get a unique sense of acheivement that no any other course could offer.

comment_grading:
I dont know the exact range of each letter grade, but I believe A range may have around 20%, which is really a good one in UST, as a fundamental course.

Detailed grading policy:
Laboratory exercises - 10%
Programming assignment 1 - 12%
Programming assignment 2 - 13%
Midterm examination - 25%
Final examination - 40%

If you attend every class and laboratories, getting full scores in exercises and programming assignments is easy.

But I hate examinations. You need to write codes on paper, not on computers, which you might feel weird and uncomfortable, and eventually make your exams scores bad.

I got a 79 out of 100 for final score, and got a B+.

comment_content:
Covered basic python programming syntax and concepts in 3/4 of the term. Then start teaching numpy/pandas/matplotlib in the remaining 1/4 term. I think the teaching on numpy/pandas/matplotlib are quite accelerated and inadequate in teaching time, especially when topics of other courses are getting harder near final exam to squeeze your time. You probably need to self study those libraries usage before they start covering numpy

btw i think numpy/pandas/matplotlib are useless. This is probably the last time in my life using them. Pandas' design is horrible, you will never know whether data should be inputted in column-based or row-based. The way it index data is also horrible and confusing, iloc[] ??? sucks. Should be removed from the syllabus. Thats why i gonna give an E in content.

(the following parts are copied)
1. Introduction to Computers and Programming
2. Python Programming Fundamentals
- fundamental data types
- fundamental operators like +, -, *, /, assignment operators and logic operators
- short circuiting/lazy operation of and, or
- caches of small integers
3. Branching Statements
- if statements
- match case statements
4. Looping Statements
- for and while
5. Collections – container data types
- list and tuple, and their operators, slicing
- deep copy and shallow copy in list
- dict and their operator
6. Modularization – Functions and Recursions
- functions, type hint, positional argument and keyword argument
- global variables
- passing mutable object to functions
- recursions
7. Modularization - Modules, Packages, and Libraries
- functional programming using reduce, map functions
- lambda functions
8. Object-Oriented Programming
- use of class and their "public", "private", "protected" members, name mangling
9. NumPy, Pandas and Matplotlib
- NumPy, their operations and properties, and broadcasting
- introduced very minimal Pandas and Matplotlib

comment_content:
The content basically looks like this:

1. Basic Programming Language Elements
2. Python Basic Operations
3. Branching (if/elif/else)
4. Looping (for / while)
5. Collections – Container Data Types (Part I & II)
6. Functions (Part I & II)
7. Recursion
8. NumPy
9. Intro to Object-Oriented Programming
10. Pandas
11. Matplotlib

Lock in and revise or you'll fall behind.
comment_teaching:
Desmond is genuinely a good teacher, he's always energetic and makes the lectures as interesting as possible. Lectures are recorded.

The TA however, is on the opposite side of the scale. He speaks poor English and is quite hard to understand, he's clearly inexperienced, the labs are poorly organized, and can at times be boring, which made me despise attending labs.
comment_workload:
Labs are weekly and you have to answer around 4 IPRS questions each time to get attendance points, be punctual.

4 out of 5 lab exercises are counted towards the grade, labs in the beginning are quite easy but get progressively harder, though they help a lot in getting you familiar with the topics, don't miss an exercise.

The programming assignments are quite difficult and time consuming, each can take up to 30 hours including debugging time if you're a beginner, so start early.

Midterm (25%)
Final (40%)

Fair grading, about top 20% gets you into A range.

comment_content:
Course Description
This introductory course is designed to provide a comprehensive overview of the Python programming
language and its core concepts, such as data types, variables, operators, and control structures, as well as
the collection data types, including lists, tuples, sets, and dictionaries. Additionally, it covers the language's
functions and the fundamentals of object-oriented programming. The course also explores a range of useful
Python modules and packages, such as NumPy, Pandas, Matplotlib, through meaningful examples and
applications, as well as hands-on experimentation using a well-known integrated development environment
and AI tools. Furthermore, this course will discuss good programming practices and debugging skills.
List of Topics
1. Introduction to Computers and Programming
2. Python Programming Fundamentals
3. Branching Statements
4. Looping Statements
5. Collections – container data types
6. Modularization – Functions and Recursions
7. Modularization - Modules, Packages, and Libraries
8. Object-Oriented Programming
9. NumPy, Pandas and Matplotlib
comment_teaching: Desmond taught every concepts clearly and he is very passionate to answer the questions raised by the students.
comment_workload: Low for people who have learned python before, but pretty high if you havent learned it
comment_grading: Pretty fair grading. I got an A- even that I do not have any programming background before.

comment_content: The content is pretty good. It contains basic python syntax, functions and some interesting modules. But there is a small problem which is the course pay a lot of time on basic syntax and functions, leading to there is no enough time to go through the subsequent knowledge, such as recursion and Numpy I find more challenging.
comment_teaching: Cecia is a really good teacher!!!!!! She will explain every concept in detail during classes, and will make difficult knowledge easier to understand. Besides, she will answer your questions patiently if you ask her questions after class.
comment_workload: If you are a beginner, this course won’t be easy to you. Because you need to memorize and understand all concepts and know how to use different functions appropriately. What’s more, just know about basic knowledge is not enough, sometimes you need to think whether there are other ways to solve same problems. There are also a lot of details you need to memory and distinguish. However, I believe in this course, the effort you pay are proportional to the results you get. Don’t be afraid or unconfident just because you are a beginner!
comment_grading: The grading seems to be pretty good. I don’t know the accurate data, but there are about 25% A range.

comment_content:
1. Introduction
2. Basic Programming Language Elements
- very basic stuff like main(), data types, input()
3. Python Basic Operations
- just symbols like these: + - * / % // == >= !=
4. Branching (aka Checking & Selection)
- use of if-elif-else, match-case
5. Looping Statement
- for loop, while loop, for-else loop, while-else loop
6. Collections - Container Data Types (Part I)
- list, list slicing, shallow copy, deep copy, 2D list
7. Functions (Part I)
- define a function, call a function, also teach some algorithm with if-else
8. Functions (Part II)
- global variables, list passing, parameter passing, 3 lambda functions: filter(), map(), reduce()
9. Collections - Container Data Types (Part II)
- tuple, dictionary
10. Recursion
11. Numpy
12. Introduction to Object-Oriented Programming
13. Pandas
14. Matplotlib
comment_teaching:
Cecia is a good tutor. Desmond is even better. The teaching team (including TAs) are supportive and reply to our question on Piazza very fast.
But I want to point out that reading ppts is not enough for you to learn Python. Spend some time on Youtube watch some Python tutorial video and try writing some simple codes on your own. It would definitely help you to memorize the syntax and functions.
If you think the content is easy, go self learn the difficult topics like Numpy, Pandas, Matplotlib, oop.
comment_workload:
If you know some Python, you don't really need to study the mid-term. For those who knows nothing about Python, if you don't work hrad, you are cooked. PA1 was said to be too difficult for beginners. PA2 involved really difficult oop stuff and some numpy. It really take some times to debug while doing PA. AI is always a good helper, though it may consider as plagiarism.
Final exam is tough as the 3 libraries Numpy, Pandas, Matplotlib is just a whole bunch of functions. You need to memorize some function names and know what are the parameters. The questions are also challenging, I have no idea how ppl get a 97.25/100.

comment_content:
The content is quite difficult if you don’t have any programming experience. The exams are really hard (at least for me it was) and basic understanding of python will not do you much good. On top of that, it a mandatory course for some non-CS majors so I would say it is going to very difficult for them.

It starts off rather easy. However, soon recursion, numpy, pandas and mathplotlib are introduced in a very short period of time. These topics are quite challenging and difficult to understand.
comment_teaching: The lecturer I had was really good. He tried his best to engage with students and made an effort to know about you. He will give you advice appropriately and guide you if you are struggling. However some of the TAs for the mandatory labs are very bad. The main TA for my lab was good, but sometimes he was replaced by other TAs who are terrible at teaching.
comment_workload: Workload of the course is manageable but self-study is crucial for this course. You will have labs due every 2 weeks with 2 programming assignments (one before midterm and one before finals). The labs and programming assignments are easier compared to the exams. It is possible to get full makes on them just don’t cram the night before they are due since you need to fine-tune them.
comment_grading:
I have to say grading is very good. Even if you do bad in your exams you will have 2 programming assignments, 10 labs and lab attendance to bring your grade up. Try to keep full marks on the other things. Just attend the labs and answer the IPRS questions (you don’t even need to get them correct just answer them). Lectures aren’t mandatory but labs are.
Grading is:
10%- labs+lab attendance
25%- programming assignments
25%- midterm
40%- finals

Try not to take the course at the same time as CSE students because they are more likely to know python before this course and even though grading is generous (approx. 25% A range), it is hard to get it. Mid term and exam are mostly based on writing code on paper by hand, so it favours people who are experienced as it is likely for newer coders to make simple syntax mistakes.

comment_content:
1. Introduction to Computing and Programming Basics
   Overview of computers, basic programming concepts, and the purpose of programming languages.

2. Core Python Fundamentals
   - Basic built-in data types (int, float, str, bool, None)
   - Essential operators: arithmetic (+ − * /), assignment, comparison, logical (and, or, not)
   - Short-circuit (lazy) evaluation in and / or expressions
   - Small integer caching / interning behavior

3. Decision Making – Conditional Execution
   - if, elif, else statements
   - Structural pattern matching with match … case

4. Repetition – Loops
   - for loops (including iteration over sequences)
   - while loops
   - Loop control: break, continue, else clause on loops

5. Container / Collection Data Types
   - Sequences: list and tuple – creation, methods, operators, slicing
   - Shallow copy vs deep copy (especially with nested structures)
   - Dictionaries: creation, access, modification, common operations, dictionary views

6. Code Organization I – Functions & Recursion
   - Defining functions, parameters, return values
   - Type hints / function annotations
   - Positional arguments, keyword arguments, default values
   - Variable scope: local vs global (global / nonlocal keywords)
   - Passing mutable vs immutable objects to functions
   - Recursion: base case, recursive case, call stack behavior

7. Code Organization II – Modules, Packages & Functional Tools
   - Creating and importing modules
   - Packages and the role of __init__.py
   - Lambda (anonymous) functions
   - Higher-order functions: map(), filter()

8. Object-Oriented Programming in Python
   - Defining classes and creating instances
   - Instance variables and methods
   - public and "private" naming conventions
   - Name mangling (__variable) and its purpose

9. Python Library – NumPy, Pandas and Matplotlib
   - NumPy basics: ndarrays, vectorized operations, shape, axes, broadcasting
   - Very basic Pandas: Series, DataFrame, simple selection and filtering
   - Minimal Matplotlib: line plots, scatter plots, basic figure customization(Taught in tutorial)

comment_content:
The course taught basic python concepts: types, control flow, functions, collections, classes, etc, coupled with introduction to popular python packages (numpy, pandas, and matplotlib).

Course content on python concepts were very approachable and their assignments weren't too difficult. The problematic part was the python packages: each (of three!) have their own quirks and mechanisms (e.g. numpy's indexing and broadcasting) that has nothing to do those previously learnt python concepts. Each package is a whole new surface to cover, yet they were all squeezed into just the last few lectures. The course should've allocated more time for students to digest this last part.
comment_teaching:
Cecia Ki was a good instructor; attending the lectures was genuinely more fruitful than reading the slides by myself.

comment_workload: There were 5 lab assignments and 2 programming assignments, spaced out over the whole semester. In my experience the harder lab assignments took a little over an hour to complete. Programming assignments were more complex and took one/several afternoons. The assignments were always posted several weeks before the deadline so there was always enough time to work on them.

comment_content: The course content is well-structured and starts from the absolute basics, making it truly accessible for beginners. It systematically progresses to more complex topics. While the concepts themselves are not inherently "hard," the pace can be challenging as it requires quick assimilation and consistent practice to keep up.
comment_teaching: The teaching quality is a highlight of the course. Instructors are typically clear, engaging, and supportive. They explain concepts thoroughly and are responsive to questions during lectures and office hours. The use of practical examples helps solidify theoretical understanding.
comment_workload: The workload is substantial. It includes regular programming assignments, quizzes, lab work, and a final project. Some assignments, particularly those involving integrated concepts or external libraries (like numpy which is crazy hard for beginners), can be difficult for beginners, demanding considerable time for research, trial-and-error, and debugging.
comment_grading: The grading scheme is generally considered fair and lenient in terms of marking criteria. However, the course curve can feel intense due to the presence of many highly skilled students (including those with prior programming experience or exceptional aptitude). For a true beginner, achieving a top grade requires significant effort and proactive learning.

comment_grading:
The course uses "relative grading". (according to unofficial data, around 70% of students get B-A range)
It counts scores in 4 area:
Lab(including attendance(all labs) and exercise x4 out of 5)
PA(programme assignment)x2
Mid-term
Final

Both Labs and PAs provide shown test cases(50%) and skeleton codes, so it is possible to understand what it requires u to finish. There are also 50% of hidden test cases to ensure ur code is reasonable instead of by luck or hard coding. Both of them provide unlimited attempts before deadlines so u can try as many times as possible to get at least 50% marks. They do take u time to understand and finish so finish them asap is recommended. For Labs, PA1 and PA2, I got 100%, 95% and 100% respectively. So they are possible for beginners like me to finish those assignments.

Since the contents for mid-term are covered in secondary school, I found that it is impossible for me to be above upper quartile. Yet, having the new topic numpy, most ppl learnt from the beginning like me. Listening to lecturer's teaching and asking tas questions, I was above the upper quartile in the final.

The exam curve might seem to be terrifying, but it is possible for beginners to get a good grade. I got an A- in this course which was out of expectation.

comment_content:
No turtle anymore

1. Introduction to Computers and Programming
2. Python Programming Fundamentals
- fundamental data types, mutable objects and immutable objects
- fundamental operators like +, -, *, /, assignment operators and logic operators
- short circuiting/lazy operation of and, or
- caches of small integers
3. Branching Statements
- if statements
- match case statements
4. Looping Statements
- for and while
5. Collections – container data types
- list and tuple, and their operators, slicing
- deep copy and shallow copy in list
- nature of list and tuple: list are mutable, tuple are immutable
- dict and their operator, immutable keys
- list/tuple/dict comprehension (useful for one-liners)
6. Modularization – Functions and Recursions
- functions, type hint, positional argument and keyword argument
- global variables
- passing mutable object to functions
- recursions
7. Modularization - Modules, Packages, and Libraries
- functional programming using reduce, map, filter functions
- lambda functions
8. Object-Oriented Programming
- use of class and their "public", "private", "protected" members, name mangling
9. NumPy, Pandas and Matplotlib
- NumPy, their operations and properties, and broadcasting, fancy indexing, slicing
- introduced very minimal Pandas and Matplotlib

PS: Talking about the notorious NumPy, it seems to be included to connect the content of COMP2211, so if you don't plan to take COMP2211 in your later semesters, it's quite natural to think that module is useless, but whatever this course tries to provide a smooth transition to COMP2211 and that's the aim of this course, so I won't say sth like teaching this module in this course is bad, but it's true that this course's content and schedule are quite immature, with a rush teaching in numpy and shift quite a long time in teaching basic syntaxes. I believe the details of its immaturity is being emphasized a lot in many other reviews.

PS: Some people are just complaining the limitation of prohibiting the use of for, while loops in NumPy question. I would say if for, while loops are not prohibited, there is no means to introduce NumPy to reduce the running time, so only allows using NumPy functions and limiting the use of loops is reasonable to encourage you to understand NumPy arrays more, but not treating it as another version of nested lists....
comment_teaching: only attended Alex's lectures in first week
comment_workload:
As a introductory cs major course, the workload of this course is quite fair.
Lab exercises do not require a lot of times to finish, while for PA1 and PA2, I used 1.5 hours and 4 hours respectively to finish.

Midterm is an exam emphasizes hand-writing code (and some problems requires you to write in oneline) so for some people who are new to programming, this exam will be a bit rush and difficult.
While Final exam shifts its majority question types to ask the output of some codes, fill in the blanks on a semi-finished code, and some questions asking you where is the error, which is similar to COMP1021. However, the coding part still take a large portion (~55%?) in the exam paper and NumPy questions are quite hard, so I recommend you to take Python Proficiency Test first to have a look of those question types and put more efforts on practicing NumPy.

If you didn't learnt python before, the course may be a little tough to you.

comment_content:
I would say this course is good for those who study python or other programming language before. For beginners, I think this course really need to spend more times for studying and reviewing if you want to get a good grade or learn something.
This course mainly involve numpy and matplotlib, which I think is really useful for python starting
comment_teaching: Desmond is really a A++ teacher, he always provide energetic lectures ( even it's a 900 am course), and you can always be free to ask them via gmail or f2f.
comment_workload:
I wouldn't say it's some easy course, if you're a beginner, you really need to pay effort in order to learn things. However, if your time management is good enough, actually there will be 1 lab exercise every week, and around 1 month for every program assignment.
I use 1 hour to do every lab exercise
1.5 - 2 hour to do program assignment
those exercises aren't hard, you can search online or ask copilot( an agent in vscode) to get the answer. HOWEVER, remember not to copy directly, but learn it, ELSE YOU WILL BE VERY SAD IN EXAMS.
